coredata.dc:title: Polyaniline nanofiber/carbon dot nanohybrid as an efficient fluorimetric sensor for As (III) in water and effective antioxidant
coredata.dc:creator: Saikia, Aditi
Karak, Niranjan
coredata.dc:description: We report a facile in-situ fabrication of polyaniline nanofiber/carbon dot nanohybrid as an efficient fluorimetric sensor for As (III) up to 0.001 ppb. The fabricated nanohybrid was characterized by various instrumental techniques such as Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR), Raman, UV–vis (UV–vis), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). XRD studies showed (002) plane of carbon dot, and (010) and (110) planes of polyaniline in the nanohybrid. UV–vis absorption at 279, 439 and 776 nm and fluorescence emission at 434 nm were also observed for the nanohybrid. HRTEM analysis revealed the formation of the polyaniline nanofibers with average diameter of 40–55 nm and d–spacing of 0.380 nm. The nanohybrid also acts as a good antioxidant as it can scavenge up to 86% of 2,2-diphenyl picryl hydrazyl (DPPH) radicals within 30 min. The results clearly indicated that the studied nanohybrid has great potential for fluorimetric detection of As (III) in contaminated water and as an effective antioxidant.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorimetric sensing
Arsenic (III) detection
Polyaniline/carbon dot nanohybrid
Photoluminescence
Radical scavenger

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Methods of preparation for carbon dot and its nanohybrid with polyaniline nanofiber
Method of detection for heavy metal

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra of PN, PNCD 3, CD; (b) Raman spectra of PN and PNCD 3; (c) UV–vis. spectra; PL spectra of (d) CD; and (f) PN and PNCD 3 at different excitation wavelength.
(a) XRD patterns of PN, PNCD 1, PNCD 2 and PNCD 3; and EDX maps of (b) PN and (c) PNCD 3.
(a) and (b) HRTEM images of PNCD 3 showing fringes as inset; (c) SEM image of PNCD 3; (d) and (e) HRTEM images of PN; (f) and (g) HRTEM images of CD showing fringes; and (f) SEM image of PN.
(a) Thermal study of PN and PNCD 3 (b) PL spectra of sensing of different metal ions at 2.000 ppb concentration and (c) DPPH scavenging study with different concentrations of PNCD 3 and PN.
PL spectra of As (III) sensing by (a) PNCD 1, (b) PNCD 2, (c) PNCD 3; and (d) Hg (II), (e) Cd (II) and (f) Pb (II) sensing by PNCD 3 at different concentrations.

PL spectra for detection of As (III) by (a) PN and (b) CD at different concentrations; (c) PL spectra for As (III) detection at different pH in 2.000 ppb concentration; (d) PL spectra for As (III) detection in unknown samples; (e) Plot of fluorescence intensity vs concentration of As (III) and (f) Linear correlation plot showing the concentration of unknown samples (U1 and U2).
Domain length (L), d- spacing and strain (ε) of PN, PNCD 1, PNCD 2 and PNCD 3.
CHN content of PN and PNCD 3.

head.ce:title: Polyaniline nanofiber/carbon dot nanohybrid as an efficient fluorimetric sensor for As (III) in water and effective antioxidant

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Polymer and Nanomaterial Laboratory, Department of Chemical Sciences, Tezpur University, Napaam 784028, Assam, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a facile in-situ fabrication of polyaniline nanofiber/carbon dot nanohybrid as an efficient fluorimetric sensor for As (III) up to 0.001 ppb. The fabricated nanohybrid was characterized by various instrumental techniques such as Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR), Raman, UV–vis (UV–vis), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). XRD studies showed (002) plane of carbon dot, and (010) and (110) planes of polyaniline in the nanohybrid. UV–vis absorption at 279, 439 and 776 nm and fluorescence emission at 434 nm were also observed for the nanohybrid. HRTEM analysis revealed the formation of the polyaniline nanofibers with average diameter of 40–55 nm and d–spacing of 0.380 nm. The nanohybrid also acts as a good antioxidant as it can scavenge up to 86% of 2,2-diphenyl picryl hydrazyl (DPPH) radicals within 30 min. The results clearly indicated that the studied nanohybrid has great potential for fluorimetric detection of As (III) in contaminated water and as an effective antioxidant.

ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity due to contamination of heavy metals like cadmium (Cd), lead (Pb), arsenic (As), chromium (Cr) and mercury (Hg) is posing a serious threat to the environment and health. Amongst all these heavy metals, arsenic contamination is causing more toxic effects to human as well as animal health due to its easy availability in soil and water. The toxicity of As is dependent upon its chemical nature and oxidation state. It is present in nature in +3 and +5 oxidation states. However, according to the several research groups the +3 oxidation state of As is more toxic compared to +5 oxidation state [1,2]. The limit of As (III) in drinking water permitted by the World Health Organization (WHO) is a maximum of 10 ppb but it is approximated that the available As concentration in groundwater in South and South-East Asia is more than 10 ppb [3,4]. Consumption of such As contaminated drinking water causes arsenicosis which results into fatigue, skin and bladder cancer, hyperkeratosis on the palm and feet as well as results into mutagenic disorder. The toxic effects of As (III) can sustain even at very low concentration as it is non biodegradable and can enter into the biological cycles like Kreb’s cycle leading to serious health hazards [5,6]. Several conventional and advanced techniques such as fluorescent spectroscopy, UV−Vis spectroscopy, atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma/atomic emission spectrometry, voltammetry, colorimetry, electrochemical techniques, SERS spectroscopy etc. were developed for detection of As including other heavy metals [7–11]. In addition to this, myriad of continuous efforts were made to develop different sensing materials with improved sensing efficiency. In this vein, literature advocates the utilization of different types of nanomaterials such as carbon dot (CD), graphene, metal oxide nanoparticles, polymeric nanomaterials etc. which enhances the detection limit because of their ultra-sensitivity and high selectivity and thereby allowing fast and efficient detection of the metal ions [12–14]. Amongst them, commonly used nanomaterial is polyaniline nanofibers (PN) containing aromatic conjugated system with π-π electronic structure which exhibits absorption in both ultraviolet as well as in the visible range and hence can be employed for sensing applications. Furthermore, low-cost, easy preparation, low toxicity, redox activity and favourable conductivity of these nanofibers offer wide acceptability [15,16]. On the other hand, PN based sensing films suffer from some serious limitations such as brittleness, non responsive on increasing thickness, low sensing ability etc. [15]. Virji et al. reported the use of different metal salts–polyaniline composites for the detection of arsine (AsH3) gas by monitoring the change of resistance. However, the technique involves tedious calculations and is expensive as well as the used metal ions are non degradable and hence contaminate the environment after disposal [17]. In this context, CD has attracted considerable attention to the researchers because of their advantageous properties like inherent luminescence, biocompatibility, water solubility, low cost, easy preparation, resistant to photobleaching, high photostability etc. Thus it finds application in bioimaging. Therefore, it may also be utilized as a sensing material for detection of heavy metals [18,19]. In this milieu, the presence of peripheral polar groups on the surface of CD along with the semiquinoid structure of PN may be effectively utilized for interaction with the heavy metals and thereby sensing them efficiently. Wang et al. report the combination of CD and PN showed good sensing property that can be used as FRET (Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer) sensors [20]. However, the study can only detect up to 0.5 μM (0.5 ppm) concentration of Hg (II) ions which is much higher than the permissible limit suggested by WHO (10 ppb). This low sensitivity may be due to the poor interaction between the components, as the physical mixture was used. Thus, in the present study, a facile protocol was employed to obtain in-situ generated PN/CD nanohybrid, which is expected to exhibit improved sensitivity for detection of heavy metals. The system may also possess interesting photoluminescence behaviour and thereby the metal ions can be easily detected by fluorescence spectroscopy.

Therefore, the authors wish to report a facile preparative protocol for fabricating PN/CD nanohybrid as As (III) sensor, for the first time. The structure of the fabricated nanohybrid was also well characterized by using different spectroscopic, microscopic and diffraction techniques. The nanohybrid was used for fluorimetric detection of different heavy metals, especially As (III) at very low concentration. The thermal and scavenging properties of the nanohybrid were also evaluated.

Aniline (Sigma Aldrich, Germany) was freshly distilled with Zn metal dust under vacuum prior to use. Ammonium peroxodisulfate (APS) (Merck Specialities Pvt. Ltd., India), hydrochloric acid (HCl) (Merck Specialities Pvt. Ltd., India) and sodium hypochlorite (Merck Life Sciences Pvt. Ltd., India) were used without any further purification. Sodium arsenite (Sigma Aldrich, Germany), lead nitrate (Merck Specialities Pvt. Ltd., India), mercury acetate (Sigma Aldrich, Germany) and cadmium chloride (Merck Specialities Pvt. Ltd., India) were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods of preparation for carbon dot and its nanohybrid with polyaniline nanofiber
ce:section.ce:para: At first, CD was prepared from rotten tomato by using a similar hydrothermal method as reported earlier by De et al. [21]. In brief, a rotten tomato was grounded into a fine paste and 5 g of the paste was taken in a beaker. An amount of 25 mL of 10% aqueous ethanol solution was added into the paste along with a few drops of fresh lemon juice. The mixture was then transferred to an 80 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave, heated at 150 °C for 6 h followed by cooling to room temperature. Then, 10 mL of water was added into it and the residue was filtered and collected. The yellowish brown filtrate exhibited characteristic fluorescence of carbon dot in dispersed phase under UV light. This CD was subsequently used for the preparation of the desired nanohybrid.

PN/CD nanohybrid was prepared by the following method [22]. In this approach, 0.2 M aniline and 0.25 M APS were prepared separately in 1 M HCl aqueous solution. A 100 mL three-neck round bottom flask placed over an ice bath (0–5 °C) with 20 mL of above aniline solution and 0.99 g of prepared CD by maintaining 1:3 wt ratio. The mixture was stirred and then, 20 mL of above APS solution was added into it. An amount of 2 mL of 5 wt% sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution was added as the reaction mixture starts to turn brown, immediately after that the stirring was stopped. The reaction mixture was allowed to stand for 30 min. The solid residue was then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 7 min followed by washing with 1 M HCl, distilled water and finally with acetone. The residue was obtained as acetone dispersion which can be dried at 80 °C for 24 h. This in-situ generated dark green nanohybrid is coded as PNCD 3. Similarly, PNCD 1 and PNCD 2 were also prepared by following the same procedure using the weight ratio of aniline to CD as 1:1 and 1:2 respectively. For comparison purpose, PN was also prepared using the above mentioned procedure with the same reactants except CD under similar conditions.

The physical mixture of PN and CD in the aqueous medium at same concentration as above was also prepared by mixing them using mechanical stirring followed by ultra-sonication. The solid residue was separated by centrifugation followed by washing with 1 M HCl, distilled water and finally with acetone. However, the residue did not show any characteristic signature of CD in ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrum. This clearly indicates the complete removal of CD from the mixture and hence it was not further studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method of detection for heavy metal
ce:section.ce:para: The nanohybrids viz. PNCD 1, PNCD 2 and PNCD 3 were used for the fluorimetric detection of As (III) by measuring the luminescence emission intensity. At first a mother solution containing 1 mM As (III) in 10 mL of distilled water was prepared. This solution was then diluted to different concentrations of As (III) namely 0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.50, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 ppb. Then, 0.027 g of PNCD dispersed in 0.5 mL of acetone was added to each of these bottles and mixed well. The fluorescence spectra corresponding to each of these concentrations were recorded and their respective intensity with respect to the intensity of each of the nanohybrids was calculated. The similar procedure was followed for CD and PN which were tested for control study. Also, the different buffer solutions of As (III) of concentration 2.000 ppb were prepared at different pH in order to check the effect of pH on sensing ability of PNCD 3. Further, this method was validated by detection of As (III) in three different groundwater samples obtained from the local area. The detection study was performed following the same procedure as above where 2 mL of each unknown samples were taken and PNCD 3 was added into it and coded as U1 and U2.
The sensing study was also performed using some other heavy metal ions like Cd (II), Pb (II) and Hg (II) under the same conditions following the same procedure.

The radical scavenging assay of the nanohybrid PNCD 3 was performed by using 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH). For this study, 5 mL of 0.1 mM DPPH ethanolic solution was vortexed and kept in a dark place for 30 min. Then the absorbance of the solution was taken at 517 nm. Finally, the scavenging property of the nanohybrid in DPPH was checked by mixing PNCD 3 at different concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 2.0 mg. The percentage of scavenging was measured by using the following formula:
Scavenging efficiency = [(Ac − As)/Ac] × 100
where Ac and As are the respective absorbance of the control and the sample at 517 nm [23].

The presence of different functional groups in the nanohybrids, PN and CD was confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectral (FTIR) analyses. The FTIR spectra were recorded by a Nicolet (Madison, USA) FTIR impact 410 spectrophotometer using KBr pellets. The X-ray diffractogram (XRD) of the nanohybrids and PN over a range of 2θ=10–90° was recorded by Rigaku X-ray diffractometer (Miniflex, UK). The Raman spectra of them was recorded using a Renishaw Basis Series with 514 Lasers spectrophotometer Reinshaw, UK. The photoluminescence (PL) was studied by using fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). The thermal degradation pattern of the samples was analyzed by using TGA 4000 (Perkin Elmer, USA) thermal analyzer, heating rate of 10 °C min−1, with a nitrogen flow rate of 30 mL min−1. The surface morphology of the nanohybrids was analyzed by scanning electron microscope (SEM) JSM 6390LV (JEOL, Japan). Also, transmission electron microscopic (TEM) analysis was performed by a TECNAI G2 20 S–TWIN (FEI Company, USA) electron microscope operating at a voltage of 200 kV. UV–vis spectrum of the nanohybrid was studied by UV–vis Evolution 300 spectrophotometer (Thermofischer, USA).

The FTIR spectra of PN, CD and PNCD 3 are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The characteristic stretching frequencies of PN were found at 3446, 2923, 1625, 1562, 1458, 1303, 1075, 778 and 696 cm−1. A strong peak at 3446 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching frequency of NH and the peak at 2923 cm−1 corresponds to asymmetric CH stretching. The CN and CN stretching frequencies were observed at 1625 and 1303 cm−1 respectively. The stretching frequencies of CC stretching of the benzoid and quinoid rings for PN were marked at around 1458 cm−1 and 1562 cm−1 respectively. The stretching frequency at 1075 cm−1 is due to CC stretching for quinoid ring. The peaks marked at 778 and 696 cm−1 are due to the CH and CC stretching of benzoid ring [24,25]. Similarly, the peaks at 3436, 2086, 1637, 1123 and 726 cm−1 are assigned for the characteristic stretching frequencies of CD. A broad peak at 3436 cm−1 is due to OH stretching. The peak at 1637 cm−1 is assigned for the CC stretching frequencies. The frequencies at 1123 cm−1 and 726 cm−1 correspond to CO stretching and CH bending modes [21,26]. The characteristic frequencies of PNCD were observed at 3400, 3195, 1726, 1638, 1615, 1302, 1058 and 736 cm−1. A broad peak with a shoulder at 3400 and 3195 cm−1 corresponds to overlap of both OH and NH stretching frequencies which demonstrates a notable difference between PN and PNCD. The CO, CN, CC and CN stretching frequencies appeared at 1726, 1638, 1615 and 1302 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra. The stretching frequencies at 1058 and 756 cm−1 correspond to CC and CH stretching of quinoid and benzoid rings respectively. Thus, the overall stretching frequencies of PNCD correspond to combination of the stretching frequencies of CD and PN. The Raman spectra of the PN and PNCD 3 are shown in Fig. 1 (b). The spectrum of PN was found to be in accordance with the earlier reported data [27]. The peaks observed at 1613, 1515, 1337, 1249, 1185, 836, 723 and 584 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrations of CC stretching of semiquinoid ring, CN stretching of quinoid ring, CN stretching of polaronic units, CC stretching of benzene, CH bending of benzene, ring deformation in emeraldine salt, imine deformation of quinoid and ring deformation of benzene respectively [27]. The characteristic peaks of PNCD 3 are also assigned. The peaks at 1599, 1339, 1242, 1174, 825, 716, 635 and 578 cm−1 are assigned for CC stretching of semiquinoid ring, CN stretching of polaronic units, CC stretching of benzene, CH bending of benzene, ring deformation in emeraldine salt, imine deformation of quinoid, in plane CH wagging and ring deformation of benzene, respectively [22,24,27]. These vibrations are almost same as that of PN but here a shifting of the peaks was observed. However, in addition to all these peaks two peaks were observed at 1526 and 1402 cm−1 which are due to the D and G band of CD respectively [28].

The optical properties of the nanohybrid PNCD 3 and the nanofiber PN were also investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the prepared nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 1 (c). The solution of CD showed an absorption peak at 282 nm which is attributed to the n → π* and π→π* transitions of CO and CC [29]. The absorption spectrum of PN showed two bands at around 456 and 773 nm which correspond to the respective π→π* transition and the exciton-like transition from the benzenoid rings to the quinoid rings [20,25]. However, in the absorption spectrum of PNCD nanohybrid some changes in the absorption peaks were observed. In the nanohybrid, the absorption peak of CD was observed at 279 nm, the bands for π→π* transition of benzenoid rings to the quinoid rings was moved to 439 nm and the exciton-like transition from the benzenoid to the quinoid was shifted to 776 nm. Hence, the PNCD nanohybrid showed the bands from both CD and PN indicating the presence of CD in PN [25]. Further, the photoluminescence study showed that the emission spectra of CD vary with the variation in the excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. 1 (d). From the spectra, it is noticed that the increase in the excitation wavelength resulted into the shifting of the emission peak to higher wavelength. The maximum emission was positioned at 434 nm when excited at 340 nm after which the peak intensity decreases. The photoluminescence of CD is mainly dependent upon the shape and size of the particles. Change in position of the peak with the increasing excitation wavelength is because of the size difference of CD. The band gap of CD decreases with the increasing size of the particles due to the quantum confinement effect. On the other hand, the photoluminescence of the nanohybrid was also studied by excitation from 280 to 330 nm as shown in Fig. 1(e). The studies showed strong emission peak at 469 nm on excitation at 280 nm. This emission peak arises due to the π→π* transitions of the benzoid–quinoid ring. From the spectra, it is observed that the intensity of the nanohybrid PNCD is more compared to PN due to the presence of CD which results into greater number of surface functionality and hence, greater is the intensity [31].

The XRD patterns of PN and PNCD 1, PNCD 2 and PNCD 3 are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The XRD pattern of PN showed broad reflections at around 2θ=13.3° and 24° due to the reflections of (010) and (110) planes. These are assigned to the spacing between benzoid and quinoid rings of the adjacent chains. These are indication of parallel and perpendicular orientations [30–32]. The reflections of the nanohybrids, namely, PNCD 1, PNCD 2 and PNCD 3 were visible at 2θ= 24.6, 24.8 and 25.01° for (110) plane and 2θ=13.6, 13.9 and 14.3° respectively for to (010) plane. However, the patterns of nanohybrids PNCD showed an additional reflections at around 2θ = 21° corresponding to the (002) plane of CD [21,28]. Moreover, the broad reflections at around 2θ = 24.6° and 13.3° in PN corresponding to (010) and (110) planes, respectively intensified upon increasing the proportions of CD in the nanohybrids (Fig. 2(a)) [32,33]. The intense peak at around 25° was deconvoluted using Gaussian function to calculate the domain length, strain and d–spacing of PNCD 3 and PN (Table 1 ). The variation in the domain size and strain in the crystallites may result into line broadening in the XRD patterns. The stacking and twin faults of grains along with small-angle boundaries domains of sub-grains may affect the size of coherent domains. However, the broadening of strain is usually caused by the different imperfections in the lattice such as the point defects and dislocations. Thus, this study can provide a detailed understanding about the effect of CD in the properties of PN. The d–spacing in PN was 4.02 Å, which was decreased to 3.89 Å in case of nanohybrids. This result pointed out the fact that the tilt angle of Cring NCring of the nanofibers decreases and the π–stacking between the chains of PN increases with the addition of CD to the PN chains. The value of domain size of the PNCD 3 was about 2 times higher than the PN domain size [34]. This indicates that the order of the polymer chains in the nanohybrid increases compared to the nanofiber. This is due to the increase in the interaction between the anilinium ions with the hydroxyl groups of CD. The strain of the nanohybrid was found to be lower than the strain of PN which is attributed to the decrease in the amount of defects associated within the chain of the polymer [25].

Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was also performed to determine elemental compositions of PN and PNCD 3 (Fig. 2(b) and (c)). The EDX data of PN showed the presence of C and N with weight and atomic percentages of 66.51:32.29 and 70.44 and 29.32 respectively. Similarly, the weight and atomic percentages of C, N and O in PNCD 3 were 67.25:19.15:13.57 and 71.24:17.50:10.54 respectively. The CHN content of PN and PNCD 3 were determined by CHN analysis and tabulated in Table 2 . The amount of C, H and N is in accordance with the elemental composition of PN and PNCD 3.

The surface morphology of PN and PNCD 3 were analyzed by using SEM and TEM images. The formation of PN was confirmed from these images as shown in Fig. 3 . The TEM images confirmed the presence of nanofibers both in PN and PNCD with the average diameter 60–75 nm and 40–55 nm respectively. Wang et al. also studied the diameter of the ex–situ prepared nanofiber as 40 nm [20]. However, the details such as SAED pattern and lattice spacing were not found in their study. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) micrograph showed a lattice spacing of 0.402 nm for PN nanofibers (Fig. 3(b)) which reduced to 0.380 nm (Fig. 3(e)) in case of PNCD 3 nanofibers. The interlayer spacing was found to be in accordance with the theoretically derived value from the XRD data. The decrease in the interlayer spacing is due to the interaction of the Cring NCring of the anilinium ion with the hydroxyl groups of CD. The TEM study also confirmed the presence of CD in PNCD 3 of size in the range of 6–8 nm. The HRTEM images showed the lattice spacing of 0.257 nm for CD (Fig. 3(f) and (g)).

The thermal studies of the PN and PNCD 3 were performed to check the thermal stability of the nanohybrid under elevated temperature. The study showed that the nanofiber exhibited two steps degradation pattern with peak temperatures at (i) 60 °C and (ii) 202 °C, whereas the nanohybrid showed three steps degradation with peak temperatures at (i) 60 °C, (ii) 245 °C and (iii) 554 °C (Fig. 4 (a)). The first degradation step of both PNCD 3 and PN is due to the release of moisture entrapped in the polymeric chain. The second step of degradation in both the cases is due to the release of the dopant molecules and the oligomers. However, the nanohybrid showed noticeably higher degradation temperature in the second step compared to the PN which is because of the association of the PN chain molecules with the carbonized structure of CD that offers additional thermal stability to the PN structure. The nanofiber finally showed a steady degradation pattern upto 700 °C with a char residue of 46%. The nanohybrid, on the other hand, showed a degradation at 554 °C which may be due to the detachment of other function moieties present within the matrix of the nanohybrid. The degradation of PNCD continues upto 700 °C with a char residue of about 50% [35].

The high sensitivity of the nanohybrid towards As (III) can be directly applicable to detect contamination in real water samples. Therefore, two different groundwater samples were collected from the locality of Assam, India to study the practical utility of the nanohybrid for As (III) detection. The samples were used as supplied and prepared for fluorimetric detection by the nanohybrid. The emission spectra indicate the presence of As (III) in U1 and U2, and the calculated values of F/Fo were 6.6 and 2.4 respectively (Fig. 5). The concentrations of the samples were determined from the linearly fitted standard curve and the values were found to be 1.08 and 0.111 ppb. The concentrations of the As (III) in the samples were exactly same as obtained by other standard technique (Atomic absorption spectroscopy). This clearly demonstrated that the nanohybrid is effective in the fluorimetric detection of As (III) even at low concentration.

As already stated (Section 2.3), the sensing study was also performed for other heavy metal ions like Hg (II), Cd (II) and Pb (II) as demonstrated in Fig. 4(d)–(f) at different concentrations (0.001 ppb to 2.000 ppb) by measuring the fluorescence intensity like As (III). However, the changes in intensity as measured by F/Fo a were found to be very low even at 2.0 ppb concentration (2.18, 1.75 and 2.94 for Hg (II), Cd (II) and Pb (II) respectively). The comparison spectra of these metal ions along with As (III) at 2.000 ppb concentration demonstrated in Fig. 4(b). The selectivity of PNCD towards As (III) is mainly due to the complexation and electrostatic interaction of As (III) between the positively charged nitrogen in the quinoid structure and the hydroxyl groups of CD with the arsenite molecule present in the solution as stated by Mandal et al. [40]. Thus, the effectiveness of detection was not very encouraging for other heavy metal ions which indicated that the nanohybrid cannot be used for the fluorimetric detection of these metal ions. Hence, these were not studied further.

The antioxidant potential of the nanohybrid was checked by the DPPH scavenging assay. This attribute of a material is dependent upon the presence of labile electrons and its ability to delocalize. The PNCD 3 nanohybrid showed excellent antioxidant activities which are due to the combined effect of PN and CD. The nitrogen atom of PN contains the lone pair of electrons which can be easily delocalized over the quinoid-benzoid based conjugated structure. Similarly, the oxygen atom of CD also contains lone pair and the graphitic structure can easily stabilize these electrons through similar delocalization. About 86% of the used DPPH (0.1 mM) was consumed within 30 min by the 0.1 mg of the nanohybrid, whereas 55% scavenging activity was observed by the same time using 0.024 mg of the nanohybrid. Thus, dose dependent radical scavenging ability was noticed for the studied nanohybrid as shown in Fig. 4(c). The results were found to be more efficient as reported by HCl doped polyaniline by Gizdavic-Nikolaidis et al. where 0.3 mg of the polyaniline removed 80% of DPPH in 30 min [41].

The present study rationally demonstrated an in-situ fabricated polyaniline nanofiber/carbon dot nanohybrid through a facile approach. Different spectroscopic, analytical and microscopic techniques confirmed the chemical and physical structures of the nanohybrid. The nanohybrid featured as an effective fluorimetric sensor for As (III) contamination in water up to a concentration of 0.001 ppb. Moreover, the amalgamation of carbon dot with the aromatic chains of polyaniline nanofiber not only enhances the thermostability but delocalization of free radicals also, resulting in excellent scavenging activity. Therefore, this nanohybrid might find application as an effective fluorimetric sensor for As (III) and a good antioxidant.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Arsenic toxicity and potential mechanisms of action
Oxidation and detoxification of trivalent arsenic species

Screen-printed stripping voltammetric/potentiometric electrodes for decentralized testing of trace lead

Bio-inspired colorimetric detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ heavy metal ions using Au nanoparticles
Water-soluble, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg 2+ in aqueous solution
Ammonia sensors based on sensitive polyaniline films

Polyaniline nanofiber-metal salt composite materials for arsine detection
